one_line_takeaway: HRA is a black-box universal multimodal adversarial attack that jointly regularizes image UAP optimization with historical-plus-estimated-future gradients and selects a single globally-influential text token via masking-based intra/inter-sentence importance ranking, outperforming five prior universal/image-only or image+text baselines (AdvCLIP, SGA, ETU, FD-UAP, C-PGC) in transferability across VLP models (CLIP variants, ALBEF, TCL, BLIP), datasets (Flickr30K, MSCOCO, RefCOCO+), and tasks (retrieval, captioning, grounding).
key_finding: On Flickr30K image-text retrieval with CLIP-ViT-B/16 as the source model, HRA_imp achieves ASR (R@1) of 76.63%/88.54% (I2T/T2I) transferring to CLIP-ResNet50, versus the next-best baseline C-PGC's 67.43%/74.58%; transferring to CLIP-ViT-L/14, HRA_imp reaches 52.37%/76.95% vs. C-PGC's 43.84%/59.33%. HRA_imp consistently outperforms all five baselines (AdvCLIP, SGA, ETU, FD-UAP, C-PGC) across nearly all 7 target-model columns and both retrieval directions in Table I, and HRA_ran (without importance-based word selection) still frequently beats C-PGC despite C-PGC also using word substitution, which the authors attribute to directly discovering substitution tokens from the corpus rather than mapping learned adversarial embeddings back to a predefined word library. On cross-task transfer to visual grounding (RefCOCO+, ALBEF target), HRA_imp achieves the lowest (best-attack) accuracy of 32.85/33.86/31.74 (Val/TestA/TestB) from CLIP source vs. baseline (no-attack) accuracy of 51.24/56.71/44.79, again outperforming all baselines including C-PGC. On image captioning transfer to BLIP/MSCOCO, plain HRA achieves the lowest CIDEr score (106.60 from CLIP source, 108.19 from ALBEF source) versus a 131.38 clean baseline, again the strongest degradation among all six baselines. Ablation (Table V) shows removing future-aware momentum (w/o FM) or the text attack causes the largest single-component performance drops (e.g., w/o Text attack drops ViT-B/32 I2T ASR from 54.97% to 28.34%), and replacing future-aware momentum with conventional momentum (w Mom) also reduces performance, particularly on TCL (from 99.05% down to 35.71% I2T). A computational-cost analysis reports training time of 229s (0 future steps) rising to 309/553/675/675s at 1/2/3/4 future steps on an NVIDIA L40s GPU, with 2 steps identified as the best performance/cost trade-off.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Hierarchical Refinement Attack (HRA), a black-box, transfer-based UNIVERSAL (sample-agnostic) multimodal adversarial attack against Vision-Language Pre-trained (VLP) models. It has two novel modality-specific components proposed by the authors: (1) 'future-aware momentum' for the image modality, which augments classical momentum by also incorporating an estimated future gradient (computed by taking a d-step lookahead with the current perturbation) alongside the historical/previous gradient, to regularize the PGD optimization trajectory for a universal image perturbation (UAP) and reduce overfitting/convergence to local optima; and (2) a hierarchical text-importance attack that scores word importance via masking (measuring the semantic discrepancy between masked and original image-text representations, Eq. 6), first at the intra-sentence level (per-sample top-k salient words) and then at the inter-sentence level (aggregating candidate words' influence across other sentences in the corpus), to select a single universal trigger word substituted into any input text (perturbation budget epsilon_T=1 token). Two test-time variants are defined: HRA_ran (random word replacement) and HRA_imp (replace with the most important ranked word). The UAPs, once learned on a surrogate model/dataset, are applied to new images/texts, new downstream tasks, and new target VLP models without retraining.
threat_model: Black-box, transfer-based attack: the attacker has no access to the target VLP model's architecture, weights, training data, or downstream task/fine-tuning details at attack time. The attacker trains a universal adversarial perturbation (image epsilon_I constrained by l-infinity norm, e.g. 12/255) and a universal single-token text substitution (epsilon_T=1) on a surrogate model (e.g. CLIP variants or ALBEF) and a surrogate dataset (Flickr30K), then applies the same fixed perturbation/word to unseen images and texts against different target models (CLIP with various backbones, ALBEF, TCL, BLIP), different datasets (MSCOCO, RefCOCO+), and different downstream tasks (image-text retrieval, visual grounding, image captioning) at 'reference time,' without any further model queries or retraining. This models a realistic, low-cost adversary who wants a single reusable perturbation deployable at scale rather than a per-sample attack.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation (Section V-B): the text attack inserts the same uniform word across different sentences, which remains perceptible to human readers because of the discrete nature of text, even under a strict substitution budget (only 1 token). They state adversarial transferability remains limited in some scenarios, particularly under low perturbation budgets, and note this as future work. Additional limitations evident from the results themselves: (1) the method is only evaluated in transfer/black-box settings against publicly known open-source VLP architectures, not against production or defended systems with adversarial-example detection/purification; (2) the universal text attack budget is a single token substitution, so its practical stealth against automated content filters is untested; (3) cross-task transfer (retrieval to captioning/grounding) shows 'notably reduced transferability' relative to same-task transfer, i.e., the universality claim degrades substantially across task boundaries even though HRA remains the best performer; (4) training cost analysis shows future-gradient lookahead adds real overhead (up to ~3x baseline time at 3-4 steps).

datasets_used: Flickr30K (31,783 images, ~5 captions each) - primary training/source dataset
MSCOCO 2014 (164K images, ~5 captions each) - target dataset for image captioning transfer
RefCOCO+ (141,564 referring expressions, 49,856 objects, 19,992 images) - target dataset for visual grounding transfer
quantitative_results: Table I, Flickr30K retrieval, source CLIP-ViT-B/16 -> target CLIP-ResNet50: HRA_imp ASR(R@1) I2T/T2I = 76.63%/88.54% vs. C-PGC 67.43%/74.58% vs. ETU 56.83%/61.27%
Table I, source CLIP-ViT-B/16 -> target CLIP-ViT-L/14: HRA_imp = 52.37%/76.95% vs. C-PGC = 43.84%/59.33%
Table I, source ALBEF -> target CLIP-ViT-B/32 (white-box-adjacent grey cell excluded): HRA_imp = 46.26%/59.57% vs. C-PGC = 39.75%/53.99%

Table II, visual grounding, source CLIP-ViT-B/16 -> ALBEF target on RefCOCO+ (Val/TestA/TestB), lower=better attack: baseline(no attack)=51.24/56.71/44.79; HRA_imp=32.85/33.86/31.74 vs. C-PGC=41.59/44.22/36.74
Table III, image captioning, source CLIP-ViT-B/16 -> BLIP on MSCOCO: baseline CIDEr=131.38; HRA CIDEr=106.60 vs. C-PGC CIDEr=117.33 vs. ETU CIDEr=114.81

Table V ablation (Flickr30K, CLIP-ViT-B/16 source), CLIP-ViT-B/32 I2T ASR: full HRA_imp=40.98% vs. HRA_imp w/o Text attack=20.33% vs. HRA_imp w/o Image attack=22.73% vs. HRA_imp w/o FM (future-aware momentum)=34.10% vs. HRA_imp w Mom (conventional momentum)=36.13%
Table V, TCL target I2T ASR: full HRA_imp=99.05% (as reported in Table V header row, differs slightly from Table I's 90.55%) vs. HRA_imp w Mom=35.71%
Training time by number of future gradient steps (0/1/2/3/4): 229s/309s/553s/675s/675s on an NVIDIA L40s GPU; authors identify 2 steps as best performance/cost trade-off
Image perturbation budget epsilon_I = 12/255 (L-infinity norm); text perturbation budget epsilon_T = 1 (single token substitution); PGD iterations MI=100, text-ranking iterations MT=15
baselines_compared: AdvCLIP (Zhou et al., ACM MM 2023) - image-only universal attack via neighborhood-relationship destruction
SGA (Set-level Guidance Attack, Lu et al., ICCV 2023) - image-only, aggregates gradients across mini-batches
ETU (Zhang et al., SIGIR 2024) - image-only universal attack with local utility + ScMix augmentation
FD-UAP (Wang et al., IEEE TIP 2024) - image-only universal attack focused on low-level feature disruption
C-PGC (Fang et al., ICCV 2025) - the only other image+text universal attack baseline; generates text perturbations via learned adversarial embeddings mapped back to corpus tokens
